en: - Tyler, Lynn, Ben, and Alex
So the question is, how in the world did React go from sentiments like this, to being universally loved and mostly respected?
jQuery revolutionized building for the web by both creating a simple and unified abstraction over manipulating the DOM that worked in any browser - regardless of typical browser compatibility issues.
What jQuery needed was something that encouraged a little more structure. Something that gave a backbone 😎 to our applications.

In theory, this was nice because you didn't have to worry about doing manual DOM manipulation yourself. In practice, well, implicit state changes usually lead to code that is both hard to follow and hard to debug. It also led to performance issues since Angular.js had to constantly scan your app looking for state changes.

What has changed though, is how React is used. From about 2014 - 2020, React was used (in conjunction with React Router), to primarily create single page applications. More recently, React has taken on a different role – that of a UI primitive.

I know it seems strange, but "metaframeworks" like Next.js, Remix, and Astro have gained popularity by treating React as a sort of UI primitive and building on top of it with new features like server side rendering, intelligent bundling, route pre-fetching, and more.
But regardless of how you use React, this course was designed to teach you React from first principles, so you'll be able to use it in any context.
Developers really didn't like it. And at first glance, why would they? It was drastically different than anything that had come before it.
With jQuery, what started out as a simple way to update DOM state, typically devolved into a mess of spaghetti like mutations that were both hard to predict and keep track of if you weren't careful.

You won't get very far into learning React until you hear it – React is declarative. But, what does that even mean? If you try to dive deeper, you inevitably come across a definition that looks something like this, comparing it to imperative programming.
Imperative programming is how you do something, and declarative programming is more like what you do.
The hard part about this topic is, as my friend Merrick has observed, it's one of those things you have an intuition about, but can’t seem to explain. Vibe driven development, if you will.
I’m going to ask you a question. I want you to think of both an imperative response and a declarative response.
Regardless of how I get to your house, what really matters is the car I drive. Am I going to drive an imperative stick shift car or a declarative automatic car? Enough metaphors?

1. The most obvious commonality is that they’re describing HOW to do something. In each example, we’re either explicitly iterating over an array or explicitly laying out steps for how to implement the functionality we want.
2. This one might not be as obvious if you’re not used to thinking in the declarative or even more specifically functional way. In each example, we’re mutating some piece of state.

In every example we’re describing what we want to happen rather than how (we don’t know how map and reduce are implemented, we also probably don’t care). We’re not mutating any state. All of the mutations are abstracted inside of map and reduce. It’s also more readable (once you get used to map and reduce, of course).

The imperative approach is concerned with how you’re actually going to get a seat. You need to list out the steps to be able to show how you’re going to get a table. The declarative approach is more concerned with what you want, a table for two.
By glancing at both examples, you have a clear understanding of what is going on. They’re both declarative. They’re concerned with what you want to be done, rather than how you want it done.
Here are 3 interview type questions. We're going to answer them imperatively, as if we're focused on how.

Now, let's take a look at some declarative examples. The goal is to fix all the problems from above. So each example needs to describe what is happening, can’t mutate state, and should be readable at a glance.

Well, I cheated a little bit and am using React — but note that all three imperative mistakes are still fixed. The real beauty of React is that you can create declarative user interfaces – simply by describing what you want your UI to look like based on your state.

Any time a function does anything other than this, whether through relying on state other than the input value it receives or creating an observable change to the program itself, it's said to have a side effect.
So why are side effects bad? Well, they're not bad – they're just unpredictable.
React, of course, is a lovechild of the two – giving you the declarative-ness of HTML with the feature set of JavaScript. But, what does that actually look like.
The only change we need to make it a true React component is to capitalize the first letter of our function.
The reason you need to capitalize the first letter of your component is so React knows it's a React component and not a normal DOM element – i.e. Footer vs footer.
Notice that we don't invoke Authors as if it's a normal function – i.e. Authors(). Instead, we treat it as if it were an HTML element, wrapping it in angle brackets – i.e. <Authors />.
For example, we can refactor the code above like so – assuming Timeline, Trending, and Follow are only used inside of this Home component.

For the former to be true, we'd have to be returning literal HTML. Clearly that's not the case because we can't literally have HTML inside of a JavaScript file. But if we're not returning HTML, what exactly are we returning?

For the most part, writing JSX should feel pretty natural. It's mostly just like HTML, but there are a few things to be aware of. We'll spend the rest of this post exploring some of those gotchas as well as some tips and tricks when it comes to JSX.
There also exists a shorthand syntax for React Fragment (<>), but it feels a little too magical to me, so I don't use it much.

Unfortunately, we're not quite done yet. There's just one small addition we need to make to our code. Whenever you use .map to create a list in React, you need to make sure that you add a unique key prop to each list item.

It's React's job to make rendering the list as fast as possible. When you give each list item a unique key prop, it helps React know which items, if any, change throughout different renders of that component.
For the curious amongst you, you're probably wondering how JSX actually works. We have an entire bonus section later on dedicated to this question, but here's a quick teaser.
Without the ability to pass data, in this case username, to each of our of functions, our composition would break down.
Similarly, because React relies heavily on composition, there needs to exist a way to pass data into components. This brings us to our next important React concept, props.

The primary reason for the confusion is because there's an important but subtle step between when React evaluates your code, and the browser sees it. This makes sense. The browser can only understand JavaScript – and though JSX is a syntax extension to JavaScript, it's still not browser compatible JavaScript.
The answer lies in what React calls an element.
This problem isn't anything new. We've been non-browser-compatible syntax (Elm, TypeScript, CoffeeScript, etc.) to JavaScript for years.

The primary reason for the confusion is because there's an important but subtle step between when React evaluates your code, and the browser sees it. This makes sense. The browser can only understand JavaScript – and though JSX is a syntax extension to JavaScript, it's still not browser compatible JavaScript.
The answer lies in what React calls an element.
This problem isn't anything new. We've been non-browser-compatible syntax (Elm, TypeScript, CoffeeScript, etc.) to JavaScript for years.
Let's start off with the most basic example, alerting OUCH whenever a user clicks on a button.
Next, you'll notice that we're passing our event handler as a reference to onClick, not as an invocation. Note the difference between these two onClick props.

If we pass onClick an invocation, à la handleClick(), as soon as the JavaScript engine encounters the invocation, it will invoke the function and pass the return value to onClick. This is obviously not what we want.
Instead, we want to pass onClick a reference to the function, so that it can invoke the function when the event occurs.
Next, you'll notice that we created our event handler inside the component, not outside of it. This is also a common pattern, but it may feel unintuitive at first. Can you think of why we do this?

We no longer have access to message via scope. Instead, we have to pass it as an argument to handleClick, which forces us to create another function that we pass to onClick. This indirection is unnecessary and is a good indication that we should move handleClick back inside of our component so we can take advantage of lexical scoping.
So at this point you know how to add a click handler to a button (which is cool), but let's look at a scenario that isn't straight out of a generic React tutorial.
Now let's work on our event handler that we'll pass as an prop to each of our three emojis.

This does bring up a new scenario though. We now need to know some specific information about the event that was triggered. Specifically, we need to know which emoji was copied so we can see if it was the right one.
So what does adding state to a component look like? Let's say we were building a simple counter app that keeps track of how many times a user has clicked a button.
Ironically, it won't work for the same reason, because React is just JavaScript – and that's not how JavaScript works.

Well, we need some help from React. Specifically, we need a way to tell React to preserve the value of count between different invocations of our Counter function (React refers to these function component invocations as renders).
Lucky for us, React comes with a hook that does exactly this called useState.

"Hooks are functions, but it’s helpful to think of them as unconditional declarations about your component’s needs. You use React features at the top of your component similar to how you import modules at the top of your file." - React docs
Now that we know the purpose of useState as well as how it works, let's update our Counter example from earlier to use it.

Now, regardless of how many times React renders our Counter component, the value of count will persist across all of those renders. But this brings up an interesting question, when does React render our components exactly?

If we break it down, we've seen how you create your View with JSX, and how you encapsulate that View inside of a function to get your component. But there's still one piece of the formula we need to talk about, state.

Though creating a simple Counter app is cool and gives us a gentle introduction to useState, it's not super practical. Let's look at some other use cases that'll give us more insight into how we can leverage useState in more scenarios.
It's important to note that when you pass a value to useState's updater function, whatever value you pass will always replace the current piece of state.

Instead of replacing the object, you most likely want to merge the new value with the existing state object. To do that, we can use JavaScript's spread operator to spread all of the existing key/value pairs onto a new object, then pass that new object to the updater function.
At this point our app is progressing slowly nicely. Unfortunately, it still doesn't resemble much of a todo list.
What if instead of having each Todo component manage its own state, we lift that state up to the parent TodoList component?
This would solve all of our problems. TodoList would own (and manage) the state of each todo item and then we can pass data down to components that need it via props.
The only gotcha is how you update that state. Often times when lifting state up, you decouple where the state lives from the event handlers that update that state.
So first, let's lift our todo state up to a new TodoList component and then pass it down via a prop to the Todo component.
First, we'll remove the local todo state and accept it as a prop since it now lives inside of the parent TodoList component.
Next, we'll update our event handlers to use our new handleUpdateTodo prop that we also passed down from the parent TodoList component.
Notice that handleUpdateTodo has an updatedTodo parameter. This is how we get data from where the event handlers live, in a child component, back up to where the state lives, in the parent component.
It doesn't matter where handleUpdateTodo is called, it just matters that when it's called, it receives this updatedTodo argument.
So now inside of the child Todo component, we need to make sure when we invoke handleUpdateTodo, we pass it the updated todo item.
At this point our app is in better shape than it was before, but it's still not a todo list. Let's fix that.
We'll do the same thing here by updating the state in our TodoList component to be an array of todo objects, rather than just a single todo object.
Similar to objects, whenever you want to update an array that's on state, you need to pass the updater function a new array which will replace the current array.
In this case, because TodoList is the component that owns our todos state, we'll want to create our function for deleting todo items in the same component.
Let's call it handleDeleteTodo and have it take in the id of the todo item to delete as its only argument.
Though it may have been... painstaking, hopefully at this point you feel confident with all aspects of managing state in React – including updating objects/array state as well as lifting state up.
Learning when and how to lift state up is one of the most important aspects of state management in React.
Here's the rule of thumb – whenever you have state that multiple components depend on, you'll want to lift that state up to the nearest parent component and then pass it down via props.
Whenever you're in a situation like this, what you'll want to do is create a function in the component where the state is located, and then pass that function down via props.
Then, you can pass data back up to the parent component by passing an argument to the function you passed down.
The biggest thing to notice is the relationship between handleUpdateTodo in the parent component, and handleCheckboxClick and handleEditTodo in the child component.

Though this equation gives us a simple mental model for how React works, there's one aspect of the equation that still, after all these years, seems to confuse people. Exactly when and how is f invoked? Or said differently, exactly when and how does React update the view?
To better answer this, instead of starting with how or when, let's go back one step further and start from first principles. First, what is rendering?
That naturally leads us to our next question, when exactly does React re-render a component?
With that, we now have our final , how does React actually know when the state of a component has changed? At this point it's fairly trivial and, once again, it has to do with our snapshot.

From there, if the event handler contains an invocation of useState's updater function and React sees that the new state is different than the state in the snapshot, React will trigger a re-render of the component – creating a new snapshot and updating the view.
Again, React will only re-render if the event handler contains an invocation of useState's updater function (✅) and React sees that the new state is different than the state in the snapshot (❌).

Instead, React will only re-render after it's taken into account every updater function inside of the event handler and it's sure what the final state is. So in our example, React will only re-render once per click.
Very welcoming. Notice anything peculiar about how our app works, though? Before you play with it, try to guess when our nested Wave component will re-render.
Your intuition is probably thinking never. After all, if React truly only re-renders when it absolutely has to, why would Wave ever re-render since it doesn't accept any props and has no state?

Notice that Wave re-renders whenever we click the button (changing the index state inside of Greeting). This may not be intuitive, but it demonstrates an important aspect of React. Whenever state changes, React will re-render the component that owns that state and all of its child components – regardless of whether or not those child components accept any props.

Second, the assumption that React should only re-render child components if their props change works in a world where React components are always pure functions and props are the only thing these components need to render. The problem, as anyone who has built a real world React app knows, is that isn't always the case.

Third, if you do have an expensive component and you want that component to opt out of this default behavior and only re-render when its props change, you can use React's React.memo higher-order component.
That's an exaggeration, but it does change things just a little bit.
All our examples until this point have had strict mode disabled, for obvious reasons. But so you can see it in action, here's our Wave example now in StrictMode.
Notice that every time we click the button, our app renders twice.
Your intuition might be that React will re-render for every updater function it encounters, so 3 times in our example.

Now it's uncommon, but there is a way to tell React to use the value of the previous invocation of the updater function instead of replacing it. To do that, you pass the updater function a function itself that will take in the value from the most recent invocation as its argument.
Are you having yet? Let's look at another example.

In the real world, it's not uncommon to need to do things outside of React. Fetching data from a server, interacting directly with the DOM, or even using native browser APIs like setTimeout or localStorage are all examples of real world use cases that don't fit neatly into the React's model.
A not so often talked about aspect of this formula is one simple but requisite rule in React – that the function which calculates the View needs to be a pure calculation.

Now I know what you might be thinking, surely this isn't that big of deal, right? Getting a single item out of local storage is plenty fast. Even if it slightly slows down the initial render, does it really matter?

useEffect is a hook that comes with React that, as our definition suggests, allows you to run a side effect that synchronizes your component with some outside system. useEffect works by removing the side effect from React's rendering flow and delaying its execution until after the component has rendered.

As a gentle example of useEffect, here's a component that show's the user's current battery level. We'll use useEffect since we want to synchronize the component's state with some outside system (the battery level on the user's device).
First, as mentioned earlier, our gets invoked after React renders. You can see this by the fact that Rendering gets logged before Getting battery level....
Second, and this may come as a surprise to you, by default, our effect gets invoked after every re-render. This is why the console output looks the way it does.

On the initial render we set the level state is to 0 and we log Rendering. Then, after rendering, React calls our effect which logs Getting battery level... before asynchronously fetching our device's battery level. Once it has it, if the battery level is different than our initial state of 0 (which is a safe assumption), React re-renders with the new battery level.

On the re-render, Rendering gets logged and then, after React has finished rendering, our effect is called. The effect logs Getting battery level... before asynchronously fetching the battery level, again. Since this time the battery level is the same as the previous render, our effect ends and React is done.
Admittedly, the default behavior of React calling the effect after every render might seem a little strange. In both of our examples, that's not ideal.
Because I presume you have a normal human brain, what you're probably expecting is something like this, where our second argument to useEffect tells React when to invoke the effect.
Now, whenever , React will re-run the effect.

It may seem strange, but this default makes sense if you think about it in the context of the goal of useEffect. The whole goal of useEffect is to synchronize your component with some external system. Whenever any of the dependencies that the effect needs in order to synchronize change, React, should resynchronize.

Notice that our effect only gets invoked once, after the initial render. This makes sense. We've told React that the effect doesn't depend on any values outside of the effect, so it never needs to re-run (or resync).
failed because the initial UI does not match what was rendered on the server.
To demonstrate it, let's say we were building a carousel like experience whose content was different Pokémon from the PokéAPI.
We know based on that we need to keep our network request out of React's rendering flow. Now we just need to decide where we put it, either in an event handler or in useEffect.

This becomes obvious when we break it down further. What exactly does "whenever the user interacts with our carousel" actually mean? When they click a button? What about navigating with their keyboard? What if we wanted our carousel to change Pokemon automatically every few seconds? What about on the initial render when there's no user interaction but we still need to fetch the first Pokémon?

The take away here is there's no single event that should trigger our network request. Instead, what we're really wanting to do is synchronize our component with the result of calling the Pokémon API – the perfect use case for useEffect.

Before we worry the async bits, let's build out the basic logic for our carousel first. Again, the entire premise of this app is to synchronize our component with the result of calling the Pokémon API. To do that, we'll need some state. Specifically, we'll add an id state to keep track of the id of the Pokémon we're currently viewing and a pokemon state to keep track of the actual Pokémon data. Our id state will default to 1 since that's the id of the first Pokémon, Bulbasaur.

We'll then encapsulate the async logic for fetching a Pokémon into a function called fetchPokemon. Now, whenever we invoke fetchPokemon passing it an id of the Pokémon we want to fetch, we'll get back an object with an error if there was one or a response of the Pokémon data if there wasn't.

Now the fun part. We want to synchronize our local pokemon state with the PokéAPI according to our local id state. That's a fancy way of saying whenever our local id state changes, we want to invoke fetchPokemon passing it the new id. We'll do that by calling fetchPokemon inside of useEffect and then updating our pokemon state with the result.

First, notice that our dependency array only includes one element, id. Again, this isn't telling React to re-run the effect when id changes (though, that's what happens). Instead, we're giving React an array of all of the dependencies our effect needs to re-synchronize with the outside system. As a byproduct of that, whenever React sees that id has changed between renders, it will re-run the effect.
Second, you may be wondering why we declared a new handleFetchPokemon function inside of our event. This is purely aesthetic and was done so that we could use async/await inside of useEffect.

First, we'll need to add two new pieces of state to our component, loading and error. Because fetchPokemon is invoked as a result of rendering, including the initial render, we'll initialize loading to true (and error to null).
Now we need our effect to manage our new loading and error states. Thankfully our fetchPokemon function makes this easy since it's so explicit if there's an error.

What we'll do is whenever our effects runs, reset loading and error back to their default values before we make the network request. Then, once we get back a response, if there's an error, we'll update error, and if not, we'll update pokemon before setting loading to false.

Don't get freaked out over the multiple, separate invocations of our updater functions (setError, setPokemon, setLoading). Remember, React "batches" those together intelligently so there's no wasted renders.

You could argue at this point the hardest parts are behind us. It appears we've successfully abstracted the arduous async side effect bits into useEffect, and are now synchronizing our component with the PokéAPI.

Notice the simplicity of our app now. Because we've told useEffect to synchronize our local pokemon state with the PokéAPI according to id, we've taken what has historically been the most complex part of building a web app, an asynchronous side effect, and made it an implementation detail behind simply updating id via our handlePrevious and handleNext functions.
Now, unfortunately, we're not quite done yet. As is, our code contains the worst kind of bug – one that is both inconspicuous and deceptively wasteful. Can you spot it?

Whenever we call fetchPokemon, because it's an asynchronous request, we have no idea how long that request will take to resolve. It's completely possible that, while we're in the process of waiting for a response, our Carousel updates id, which causes a re-render, which causes our effect to run again with a different id.

In this scenario, we now have two requests in flight, both with different ids. Worse, we have no way of knowing which one will resolve first. In both scenarios, we're calling setPokemon when the request resolves. That means, because we don't know in which order they'll resolve, pokemon, and therefor our UI, will eventually be whatever request was resolved last. To make it worse, you'll also get a flash of the Pokémon that resolves first, before the second one does.

Really what we want to do is to tell React to ignore any responses that come from requests that were made in effects that are no longer relevant. In order to do that, of course, we need a way to know if an effect is the latest one. If not, then we should ignore the response and not setPokemon inside of it.
Thankfully, React makes this pretty easy with the last remaining feature of useEffect you haven't seen yet: the "cleanup" function.
We can see this in action by adding a cleanup function to our effect that logs the id that the effect is associated with.

Notice that the cleanup function is only called for ids that are no longer relevant. This makes sense because the cleanup function for the most recent effect won't be called until either another effect runs (making it stale) or the component has been removed from the DOM (irrelevant in this scenario).
We can use this knowledge along with the Power of JavaScript™ to fix our problem.

Whenever the effect runs, let's make a variable called ignore and set it to false. Then, whenever the cleanup function runs (which we know will only happen when the effect is stale), we'll set ignore to true.
Then, all we have to do before we call setPokemon or setError, is check to see if ignore is true. If it is, then we'll do nothing.

Now, regardless of how many times id changes, we'll ignore every response that isn't in the most recent effect. This not only makes our app more performant, but it also improves the UX since React will now only re-render with the latest Pokémon.
You can use the document's visibilitychange event to detect when the page is no longer visible.
First, let's add some state to track the number of times the employee has tabbed away. We'll call this count and we'll also add some UI for the user to see their count.

Now the tricky part. We need to listen for the visibilitychange event and update our count state accordingly. Since we're synchronizing the document's visibility status with our component, we'll use useEffect.

First let's add our event listener. Remember, whenever you add an event listener, you also need to remove it when the component is removed from the DOM or you'll have a memory leak. useEffect's cleanup function makes this simple.

Now all we need to do is update our handleChange function to increment our count state whenever the document is no longer visible. Your first intuition might be to do something like this – where we add count to our dependency array and then increment it by 1 inside of handleChange.

Whenever the document's visibilityState is no longer visible, we increment count by one. Simple, right? Yes, but actually no. In fact, even though our app appears to be working fine, there's a major problem. Can you spot it?

Via our dependency array, we've told React that our effect depends on our count state (which is true). That means, whenever count changes, React will re-render and then, once it's done, re-invoke our effect.

We're in a weird spot. We only want React to add our event listener once, on the initial mount, but we need to include count in the dependency array because we're using it inside of the effect to calculate the new count state.

What's interesting about this example compared to our other examples is, even though we're using count in the effect, we're not using it as a way to synchronize our component with some external system – so we don't actually need React re-run the effect when it changes.

Thankfully, React has a solution for these types of scenarios and it's probably not what you'd expect. In fact, it has nothing to do with useEffect at all. Instead, it has to do with how we're updating our count state.

In both of these examples, we're telling React to increment count by 1. However, the difference is in how we're getting access to the current state of count before we increment it. In the first example, we're relying on a closure – and therefore we have to specify count inside of the dependency array. In the second, we're not, since React will pass us the current state of count as an argument and therefore, we don't need to include it in the dependency array.
With this knowledge, we can now update our example to remove count from the dependency array which prevents React from re-running our effect every time it changes.
First is how StrictMode affects useEffect. If you're not familiar, StrictMode is a component that comes with React that allows you to stress test your application in development.
When you have StrictMode "enabled" (which is the default for most React apps), React will re-render an extra time, re-run effects an extra time, and check for deprecated APIs.
Why is this helpful? You can think of StrictMode as a stress test of our 3 rules. If render is not pure or you're incorrectly managing side effects, StrictMode will (aggressively) uncover that.

It's controversial, but the whole goal of StrictMode is to make it more obvious that you're doing something wrong – which I think is a reasonable goal. If you stick to the rules we've established, StrictMode will be mostly irrelevant for you.
For more information on it, check out the official documentation.

Throughout our discussion on effects, you may have been wondering the distinction between what needs to be included in useEffect's dependency array and what doesn't. For example, why doesn't localStorage, Number, or setIndex need to be included in our Greeting effect?

The reason is because they're not reactive values. Defined, a reactive value is any value that can change between re-renders. Props, state, or any variables defined inside of a component are all reactive values – and therefore, always need to be included in the dependency array if they're used in the effect.

In our example, localStorage and Number are always going to reference the same spot in memory and React guarantees that the setIndex updater function will also always be consistent across renders, meaning all of these are non reactive values.

This makes sense if you think about it. The whole goal of useEffect is to synchronize your component with some outside system. Non reactive values are irrelevant to useEffect because they're never used in the synchronization process.
If not,

Let's walk through it. We know in order to keep track of how much time has passed, we need the right combination of component state with a side effect. Because setInterval is a side effect that's triggered by an event, we've put it inside of an event handler.
To fix this, instead of re-creating id on every render, what we want to do is tell React to preserve id across renders. Isn't this is the exact use case that useState was created for?

Yes, our app technically does work. And I'm not even mad, more just disappointed. Throughout this whole course you've been taught, nauseatingly, that in React, your view is simply a function of your state.

As is, our app breaks this mental model. We've introduced state that has nothing to do with our view, we're simply using it as a way to preserve a value across renders. That's what we in the biz call hacky.
The API is fairly simple. When you call useRef, what you get back is a ref – an object with a mutable current property whose value will be preserved across renders.
Unlike useState, this current property is and doesn't have a special mutation API. This means you can mutate it just like you'd mutate any other value, and doing so won't trigger a re-render.

You'll notice that our app is mostly fine, but there's one thing I think we can improve on. If you click on the toggle switch repeatedly, over and over, the experience breaks down. It still works, but I think the better UX is to only allow the user to toggle when the animation has finished running.
There is just one more side effect that we haven't talked about yet, and it's crucial for building on the web – interacting directly with the DOM.
This worked, but it too was brittle. If a designer tweaked how long our animations took to run, it wouldn't behave as expected.

Before we go on, I want to say that I would consider everything below this to be advanced. Don't get down on yourself if it's a little confusing at first. I've been writing React since 2014 and it even took me a little while to get a solution I was happy with.
So we're good, right? At this point in the course you probably know that if I ask that question, we are definitely not good.
Much better. We no longer have any state that isn't used to update the view, and more importantly, we're potentially React some work since our app no longer re-renders when we store the id.
To get data down the component tree, you use props. To get data up the component tree, you use callbacks.
This works... most of the time. But what happens if instead of your app looking like that, it looked like this?

Or react-intl, a library for handling internationalization and localization that needs to be able to pass locale data to any component in the component tree, again, regardless of how deeply nested the components are.

Because this is such an obvious limitation of a component based architecture, React comes with a built-in API to solve it called Context. You can think of Context as giving you the ability to teleport data anywhere in your component tree, without needing to pass props.
The one is pretty simple. You can create a Context by calling React.createContext.
Here's how it works. When you create a new Context, what React gives you is an object with a Provider property.
Provider accepts a value prop which is the data that you want to teleport to any component in Provider's subtree.
Now, because we've wrapped our entire app in Provider, any component, regardless of how nested it is, can get access to what we passed to value, marty.
To get access to what was passed to the Provider's value prop, you use React's useContext hook, passing it the Context as the first argument.
Notice that even though our Cafe80s component is a few layers deep and doesn't receive any props, we can still access the marty object via useContext.

Now admittedly there is one thing strange about this demo you may have noticed. Since marty is just a static object, why don't we just export it from a module and import it wherever we need it? There's not really a need for Context in this scenario.
With that said, let's move our example a little closer to reality by teleporting state instead of just a static object.
More specifically, let's move marty into an array, add in his friend Doc, and then teleport that array of passengers.
Note that after updating what we're teleporting to be an array, we also needed to update where we're consuming that data, in this case, the Cafe80s component.

But other than that, notice nothing else changed. One more time for those in the back, Context isn't a way to manage state, it's a way to teleport data. The "management" of our state is done by useState, not Context.
In our example, passengers is located at the root of our application. Therefor, whenever it changes, React will re-render the entire tree, passing the new passengers as the value of the Context.
Whenever you invoke useContext passing it a Context object, it'll return whatever you passed to the value prop of the nearest Provider component of the same Context.

However, what if there is no Provider component above it in the component tree? In that case, it'll get its value from the first argument that was passed to createContext when the Context object was created.
Now, if we invoke useContext(delorean) without previously rendering a <delorean.Provider> component, the return value will be the array we supplied to createContext.
Notice that our app still works the same, even though we no longer have state inside of our App component.
Admittedly this isn't very practical since now our app will never re-render if passengers changes, but it does demonstrate how to use the default value of createContext.

In reality, you'll almost always just duplicate the data – once for the default value and once for when you set it to state. Doing this assures that useContext will be able to supply a value if it doesn't have an associated Provider higher up in the tree, but also that your app will re-render when the value on state changes.
Here's a very clever example my good friend chantastic came up with for a situation in which you can take advantage of this. I've modified it a bit, but the core idea is the same.
Can you follow what's going on? First, we create a new Context and set its default value to shit. Then we render two components, VisitFriendsHouse and VisitGrandmasHouse.

Because we're not allowed to swear at our Grandma's house, we use expletive.Provider to supply a value of darn as the value we're teleporting. That way, inside of ContextualExclamation, when useContext looks for the value of its nearest Provider, it'll find it, darn.

Unlike at Grandmas, with our friend, we're allowed to swear Grandma can't hear us. So instead of wrapping it in expletive.Provider, we leave that off so that way, inside of ContextualExclamation, when useContext looks for the value of its nearest Provider, it won't find it and instead it'll use the default value, shit.

Take this scenario, for example. What you've been taught is that whenever you have state that multiple components depend on, you'll want to lift that state up to the nearest parent component and then pass it down via props.
1. Creating the teleporter Context
2. Deciding what we want to teleport and to where
3. Getting what we teleported from inside a component

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: It may seem strange, but one of the most fundamental steps on the road to mastering React is getting comfortable with the concept of referential equality in JavaScript.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: I know. If I was on a game show and had to come up with the most overcomplicated sentence I could, it'd probably contain the words referential equality in JavaScript – but I promise it's not as bad as it sounds.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: Whenever you create a variable in JavaScript, that variable can store one of two types of data, a primitive value or a reference value. If the value is a number, string, boolean, undefined, null, or symbol, it's a primitive value. If it's anything else, it's a reference value.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: If you looked at the in-memory value of a primitive, you'd see the actual value itself (28, 'Tyler', false, etc.) If you looked at the in-memory value of a reference type, you'd see a reference to a spot in memory. Or more specifically, you'd see a memory address which pointed to a spot in memory where the value was stored.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: Though a little strange, this example demonstrates an important aspect of primitive values – that the in-memory value of a primitive is the value itself. Therefore, when we assign one primitive value to another, like we're doing with displayName, what we're really doing is copying the value from one spot in memory to another.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: So even though lastName changes, and displayName uses lastName, displayName itself doesn't change because it copied the value of lastName when it was created.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: Notice the difference? When we create a new variable and assign it to a reference value (like we're doing with snoop), what we're really doing is copying the reference to the spot in memory where the value is stored.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: Then, because both values point to the same spot in memory, when we modify the value at that location in memory, it'll effect both values that reference it, snoop and leo.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: Now there's one more interesting difference between primitive and reference values and that's how the identity operator (===) compares them.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: Here we see that because name and friend have the same value, Tyler, when comparing them, we get true. This probably seems obvious but it's important to realize that the reason we get true is because, with the identity operator, primitives are compared by their value. Since both values equal Tyler, comparing them evaluates to true.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: Even though leo and leito have the same properties and values, when comparing them with the identity operator, we get false. The reason for that is because, unlike primitive values which are compared by their value, reference values are compared by their location in memory. Unless two reference values have the same location in memory, the identity operator will say they're false – and therefore, not referentially equal.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: a === b evaluates to true since both a and b point to the same spot in memory. When comparing reference values, the identity operator compares the memory address of the value, not the value itself.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: {} === {} evaluates to false since both objects are stored in different spots in memory. When comparing reference values, the identity operator compares the memory address of the value, not the value itself.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: nope === noop evaluates to false since both functions are stored in different spots in memory. When comparing reference values, the identity operator compares the memory address of the value, not the value itself.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: c === d evaluates to true since both c and d point to the same spot in memory. When comparing reference values, the identity operator compares the memory address of the value, not the value itself.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: nope() === noop() evaluates to false since both objects returned from our function invocations are stored in different spots in memory. When comparing reference values, the identity operator compares the memory address of the value, not the value itself.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: [] === [] evaluates to false since both arrays are stored in different spots in memory. When comparing reference values, the identity operator compares the memory address of the value, not the value itself.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: Now the question you've been waiting for, what does referential equality have to do with React?

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: If you remember, the way React works is that whenever state changes, it will re-render the component that owns that state and all of its child components – regardless of whether or not those child components accept any props.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev
en: If you do have an expensive component and you want that component to opt out of this default behavior by only re-rendering when its props change, you can use React's React.memo higher-order component.

file: 020 - Referential Equality and Why It Matters - ui.dev

Now I get it. Yes, we've already had a few lessons on managing side effects in React and yes, those were the longest ones in the course. Mastery is fun, effects are hard. Let's keep going.

For a microsecond, until our effect runs and updates the state, the browser dimensions are null. This means that on the initial render of our app, anywhere we use size.width or size.height won't render properly.

The primary reason this happens is because useEffect runs asynchronously after render. This is almost always what you want, as it optimizes for showing the UI to the user as quickly as possible. However, in this scenario, we actually want our effect to run synchronously before React shows anything to the user.

This is exactly the use case that React.useLayoutEffect was designed for. React guarantees that the code inside useLayoutEffect and any state updates scheduled inside it will be processed before the browser repaints the screen. This lets your component use layout information for rendering – as we're doing in our example with the browser's dimensions.

Our artificial delay is still in place, but notice that now, React doesn't show anything to the user until after its ran useLayoutEffect and updated our state. This way, the first time the user sees anything, it'll be with the layout information.
Now this works, and there's nothing wrong with it – but there is some duplication here.

Essentially all we're trying to do is subscribe to the browser's internal navigator.onLine state. However, in order to do that, we're creating and then managing another piece of state, networkStatus, inside our component. That seems redundant.

You'll notice that in our example above, both subscribe and getSnapshot live outside of our component. This is convenient because it allows us to decouple our subscription logic from our component – but there's also another, more practical benefit to it as well.
And how does React define change? The same way it always does, with .

en: Because "Don't Repeat Yourself" (DRY) is a principle of software development and not one of education, let's recap everything we know about React's useEffect hook one last time.

file: 022 - Abstracting Reactive Values with useEffectEvent - ui.dev
en: The whole purpose of useEffect is to encapsulate a side effect that synchronizes your component with some outside system. It works by removing the side effect from React's rendering flow and delaying its execution until it's safe to do so, after the component has rendered.

file: 022 - Abstracting Reactive Values with useEffectEvent - ui.dev
en: What we really need in this moment is a way to tell React that we want to use a reactive value inside of useEffect, but that reactive value has nothing to do with synchronizing the component, and therefore, shouldn't need to be included in the dependency array.

file: 022 - Abstracting Reactive Values with useEffectEvent - ui.dev
en: The biggest thing to notice is that url is still included in the dependency array and we're passing it as an argument to onPageView. This is good practice as it makes it explicit which values are synchronizing (url) and which aren't (state).

React is a library for building user interfaces. With React, your View a function of your application's State – v = f(s). All you have to do is worry about how the state in your application changes, and React handles the rest.
Now here's a question I want you to think through. Let's say we wanted to display the user's network status in another part of our app. How would you go about doing that?
What if, just like React comes with its own set of hooks, we were also able to make our own custom hooks? In that scenario, a custom useNetworkStatus hook would be awfully convenient.
One question you may have is why exactly does a hook need to start with use? That seems overly restrictive.

Now the only question left is when is it appropriate to create a custom hook? The answer, as with most things in React, is to rely on your intuition. The same intuition you've developed about functions and when to create them can be directly applied to creating hooks. Mostly, you'll know it when you see it.
A custom hook is a function that starts with use and calls other hooks inside of it

When we started working on react.gg, a primary focus of ours was figuring out how to get students comfortable working with "production" level React code, without the burden or context of needing to dive into a fully fledged project.

I'm sure you've experienced it before. In an attempt to make the course "hands-on", the instructor has you clone a starter project from Github, npm install two dozen packages, and then you spend the next 14 hours watching them glue it together.
When we built react.gg, this is the experience we wanted to recreate.
The tradeoff here, of course, is that a "real-world simulated environment" is still just that – simulated.
The course was still, despite our best effort, missing something that would allow you to get comfortable with the actual code you'll see in the actual projects you'll work on.

Then we had an idea. If we wanted you to get real-world experience, what better way than to have you build a real-world React library. Naturally, this led us to the creation of useHooks - a library of 50 modern, real-world, production ready React hooks that as of this writing gets more than 20,000 downloads a week.

What I love about useHooks, and what's impossible to do with traditional course projects, is that everything we've covered in the course so far is used in the library. There isn't one thing you've learned that you won't see again in one form or another in useHooks. Not only that, but by the nature of building reusable hooks, you'll also get more familiar with fundamental browser APIs like localStorage, matchMedia, and IntersectionObserver to name a few.
